Tafseer:
'Ilha'' means keeping someone heedless and deluded.
'Takathur' means striving to outdo one another in the accumulation of wealth and children.
The obsession with raising the standard of living: In the tribal life of Jahiliyyah, since defense and safety depended on the family and tribe, leadership belonged to the family possessing more members. This naturally made the desire for accumulating children very strong alongside accumulating wealth. Those familiar with their literature know that they boasted of their population just as they boasted of their wealth. In modern times, the changed social system and family planning have suppressed the desire for many children, replacing it with the obsession with raising the standard of living. This is the common disease of this era. Rarely can anyone be found free from its influence. Everyone is busy day and night in the race to raise their standard of living, and since there is no limit to this, every step feels like the first step, while the destination remains hidden, and no one knows where it is or when it will arrive. Since raising the standard of living depends on wealth, there is no limit to greed either. Indeed, the faster the standard of living rises, the faster the thirst for wealth increases. This is what the Qur'an has described as 'takathur', and its effect is that it has trapped everyone in its delusion, such that life passes in this pursuit and no one finds the opportunity to reflect on whether there is a life after this world, and whether any preparation should be made for it.

That is, life passes in this pursuit until your time is up and you reach the graves. 'Zurtum' is used in its simple, literal sense in Arabic, without any connotation of honor or sanctity. 'Zurtumu-l-maqabir' means: you visited the graves—meaning you died. A classical poet wrote:
'When I visited the land after a long absence,
I found my friends gone, though the land remained as it was.'
Expressing regret over the state of the lovers of the world: Although the Arabs also kept count of the graves of their named members to boast in their assemblies, this is not what is meant here, nor is it something worthy of being mentioned in this manner. However, a question arises: why was the style 'until you visit the graves' (hatta zurtumu-l-maqabir) adopted? Why was it not stated simply: 'until death comes to you'? In our view, first, the rhyme of the Surah required it; second, it expresses deep regret over the loss and deprivation of these worldly individuals—showing that they remained busy in this pursuit until they faced the graves or resided in the graveyard.

This is a highly severe warning to those heedless individuals: after everything is explained, if you are still not ready to open your eyes, remember that life is not merely what you see and what has trapped you in its love; rather, its true face is hidden from you, which you will soon see, and indeed you will see it very soon!
This repetition with emphasis is to make the style powerful, and to show that the nation that rejects its messenger faces His punishment in this world, and will face what he warned of in the Hereafter. The meaning is: if you do not change your way, you will see it in this world and in the Hereafter, and there is no delay. The court is established, and the decision is about to be issued. The warning implied in the ambiguity of 'you will know' (ta'lamun) is obvious.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 3 and 4 was combined in the original text.]

The primary cause of heedlessness: This exposes the primary cause of their heedlessness: you are in this state because you do not believe that a Day is bound to come when you will see Hell with the eyes of certainty, and then you will be questioned regarding all the blessings your Lord bestowed on you but you used contrary to His pleasure. If you possessed this certainty, you would not spend your lives in this manner, but would spend every moment in preparing for that Day.
Coherence of the text: Pondering over the coherence, the response to the conditional 'If' (law) is omitted based on context, but the subsequent verses—'You will surely see the Hellfire'—do not start a new sentence, but are grammatically subject to 'If you only knew with knowledge of certainty' (law ta'lamona 'ilma-l-yaqeen).

'You will surely see the Hellfire' (latarawunna-l-jaheem) does not start a new sentence, as commentators have generally assumed, but is grammatically subject to: 'If you only knew with knowledge of certainty'—meaning: if you knew with certainty that you will surely see Hell. The particle 'la' in 'latarawunna' is to express the certainty that is required.
This shows that the means to attain the certainty required to believe in these realities have been established by Allah Almighty in the signs of nature, within ourselves, and the clear verses of the Qur'an. Therefore, every rational person is responsible for understanding and believing. Whoever turns away from them is responsible for his own ruin, and will face punishment before Allah.

In this world, only the knowledge of certainty is attained: This also reveals that a rational person can indeed attain the knowledge of certainty ('ilm-ul-yaqeen) regarding the unseen realities in this world, because the knowledge of certainty is attained through proofs, which are explained in nature and the Qur'an. However, the vision of certainty ('ayn-ul-yaqeen) will be attained only in the Hereafter, because it relates to observation and witnessing. Those who claim that this is attained in this world are incorrect. In this world, we attain the knowledge of certainty that whatever the Qur'an informs of will be seen with our eyes one Day; and the vision of certainty will be attained on the Day we witness it.

Fulfilling the right of blessings: 'Then you will surely be asked that Day about pleasure' (thumma latus'alunna yawma'idhin 'ani-n-na'eem) - This is also subject to: 'If you only knew with knowledge of certainty'—meaning: if you knew with certainty that on that Day you will be questioned regarding all the blessings. Questioning, obviously, refers to the punishment resulting from their ungratefulness, disregard, and misuse.
All the capabilities, faculties, resources, and means Allah has bestowed on man are included in 'pleasure' (na'eem). Their natural right is to remain grateful to Him and use them within His limits for the purposes they were granted. If any blessing is wasted or used contrary to the Creator's pleasure, He must punish. Man's ears, eyes, heart, mind, and limbs are blessings; the physical and spiritual capabilities are blessings; and the resources of life are blessings. Their natural right is that man should benefit from them and remain grateful to his Lord, without transgressing His limits or becoming so infatuated with them that they become his deities, forgetting God. Those who commit such transgression will face punishment on the Day of Resurrection.
Since this Surah warns of the fitnah of accumulating wealth, it should be foremost in view here. Every owner of wealth will be questioned: from what sources did he acquire his wealth, and on what did he spend it. Those who cared neither for lawful and unlawful in acquiring it, nor for His pleasure in spending it, but made wealth their deity and spent their lives in its pursuit, will face the end described in Surah Al-Humazah:
(Woe to every slanderer and backbiter, who collects wealth and [continuously] counts it. He thinks that his wealth will make him immortal. No! He will surely be thrown into the Crusher. And what can make you know what is the Crusher? It is the fire of Allah, [eternally] fueled, which mounts directed at the hearts. Indeed, Hellfire will be closed over them in extended columns.) (Al-Humazah, 104:1-9).
Finally, the response to 'If' (law) is omitted based on context. The response is omitted where it is obvious. This has appeared previously, allowing the listener to reflect on his conduct and see how wrong, far from reality, and careless his life has been. The meaning is: if you had reflected on these obvious realities, you would not have wasted your precious life. If you desire success, use your intellect, and do not waste the life through which you can attain an eternal kingdom in accumulating the worthless pebbles of this temporary world.
